A tragic underground explosion in 1968 claimed the lives of 78 coal miners in Farmington, West Virginia, highlighting the urgent need for stronger safety measures in mining. In response, landmark mine safety standards were introduced in 1969, creating a lasting impact on worker protections. These measures: ✔️Required regular federal inspections of coal mines ✔️Gave inspectors authority to shut down hazardous mines ✔️Strengthened safety regulations and enforcement ✔️Imposed fines and penalties for violations ✔️Established health protections, including for black lung disease ✔️Provided federal benefits for those affected by black lung disease Enacted on March 30, 1970, these safety measures laid the foundation for even stronger protections in the years to come, eventually leading to the creation of the Mine Safety and Health Administration (MSHA). Read more on the history of The Federal Coal Mine Health and Safety Act of 1969 and its impacts on the lives of miners: https://www.msha.gov/federal-coal-mine-and-safety-act-1969 * Photo courtesy of the Mine Safety and Health Administration #FlaMineSafety #Miner #MineSafety #MineSafetyTraining #MineSafety #MSHA #MineRescue #MineRescueTraini …

Akshaya Patra refers to both a legendary inexhaustible vessel from Hindu mythology, and the organization's mission is inspired by the legendary vessel, aiming to ensure no child is deprived of education due to hunger. For those who are new the Akshaya Patra Foundation, a large-scale public-private partnership in India that provides nutritious mid-day meals to millions of school children daily. This organization was founded in 2000 to address childhood hunger and improve educational outcomes. The Akshaya Patra Foundation operates large, centralized kitchens, leveraging technology and partnerships to deliver meals to children in government schools and Anganwadi centers across various states in India, aligning with the Sustainable Development Goals of Zero Hunger and Quality Education. They also provide emergency food assistance during crises and support other vulnerable groups like widowed mothers and underprivileged families of young kids. Trollers work hard in this sustainable, small-boat, hook and line Southeast Alaska fishery, frequently running boats either by themselves or with a very small crew. Now, a new film showcasing troller Kat Murphy brings the viewers on board. “I wanted more people to know what it is we commercial fishermen do out there," Murphy said in the film's press release. The filmmakers followed her for a year. "We fish in all types of weather, high winds and seas, rough conditions, and long days, where the sunrises and sunsets seem to blur together into one endless day. Some years, not even getting paid enough to justify going out to sea, and yet we go, year-after-year. Why do we do this to ourselves?!…because we love it, because we are responsible for feeding people. We all take pride in respecting these fish and this resource, and I will continue to share my story, and really the story of many fishermen's lives with the public. Who is catching your protein, what people are truly paying for, and hoping people continue to see the value in that.” 🐟 🙌 Learn more about the film, including future screening events, here: www.upstreamgrace.com

🧪 This week’s new distraction (still no Epstein files?) is actually an old one. The claim that acetaminophen (Tylenol™) causes autism has been floating around for decades—and it’s just as much junk science now as it was then. As a father of an autistic child, who is now an adult, I pored over the medical literature. What I found is clear: autism has a strong genetic basis. Most of us likely carry at least one of the potential genes, and environmental triggers may play a role—but acetaminophen isn’t one of them. This drug has been the go-to pain reliever for pregnant women since the 1960s. So if it were the cause, why did autism diagnoses spike in the 1990s and 2000s? The timeline doesn’t add up. Coral diseases and nutrient pollution from land-based runoff are major threats to coral restoration efforts, particularly for staghorn and Elkhorn corals in the Caribbean. According to Science Daily, a recent study examined how different staghorn coral genotypes respond to these environmental stressors. The results showed that while some genotypes were resistant to high nutrient levels or disease individually, none were resistant to both simultaneously. The study, conducted by scientists at the University of Miami, exposed coral samples to normal and high nutrient conditions, followed by exposure to either diseased or healthy tissue. Key findings indicated that elevated nitrogen levels reduced coral survival, and the combination of both stressors led to high mortality rates across all genotypes. The research underscores the urgent need to improve water quality by limiting runoff to support coral conservation, as coral disease outbreaks often coincide with pollution-related stress, which is critical for enhancing coral resilience and the success of restoration projects. (Photo: Pewtrusts/Georgette Douwma) #CoralReefs #Conservation #Pollution https://www.pewtrusts.org/en/research-and …

Twice a year, Americans adjust their clocks—except in Arizona and Hawaii. But is daylight saving time (DST) still necessary, or is it an outdated practice that needs to go? In this lively debate, American Potential host David From sits down with Stephen Shadegg, AFP’s Arizona State Director, and Tim Parrish, AFP’s Virginia State Director, to discuss whether DST should be permanent, abolished, or left up to the states. Stephen, who has never had to change his clocks in Arizona, argues for the stability and efficiency of sticking to standard time, while Tim makes the case that DST leads to lower crime, increased productivity, and economic benefits. As Congress considers the Protect Sunshine Act to end the biannual clock changes, this episode dives into the historical reasons for DST, its impact on businesses and public safety, and whether it truly benefits the country. Should states decide for themselves, or does America need a unified time policy? Tune in to hear both sides and decide where you stand!

FUR FREE FRIDAY Fur-Free Friday began in the 1980s as a powerful grassroots movement to raise awareness about the cruelty of the fur industry. Strategically held on the day after Thanksgiving, one of the busiest shopping days of the year, it aims to educate consumers and challenge the fashion industry to abandon fur. The event has become a global protest against the exploitation of animals for fashion, shining a spotlight on the fur trade’s brutal practices, including trapping, confinement, and slaughter. Originally organized by animal rights groups, Fur-Free Friday has grown into an international call for ethical choices. Protesters, advocates, and compassionate individuals unite outside stores, malls, and public spaces to urge retailers and designers to stop selling fur. The movement has contributed to significant progress over the years, with major fashion houses like Gucci, Versace, and Prada committing to fur-free policies. At its core, Fur-Free Friday is about empowering consumers to make ethical decisions and reminding the world that animals are not commodities. It’s not just a protest; it’s a statement of compassion, demanding a shift toward cruelty-free and sustainable fas …

Residents in North Irvine Prove that Citizen Activism Works! Kevin Lien hasn’t started sleeping with his windows open again, and he doubts he ever will. But, the 19 air purifiers that the Orchard Hills resident spent tens of thousands of dollars on now sit largely unused. And, he no longer feels the need to open his front door a crack and sniff the outside air before taking his dogs for their walk. The November closure of the All American Asphalt plant in North Irvine — the result of a remarkable deal among the plant’s owners, the City and the Irvine Company — was literally and figuratively a breath of fresh air for long-suffering residents nearby and a “miracle” for a cadre of citizen activists including Lien who had been fighting the plant for five long and discouraging years. Kim Konte had already gotten her feet wet in environmental activism in 2015 when she lobbied the City to ban the use of the pesticide Roundup in Irvine’s parks and ball fields. Not only did the City ban the pesticide, but in 2016 it adopted a wide-ranging environmental policy to minimize the use of toxic pesticides and herbicides.
